coredata.dc:title: Parametric and phenomenological studies about ultrasound-enhanced biosorption of phenolics from fruit pomace extract by waste yeast
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Yang
Han, Yongbin
Liu, Wangxin
Peng, Lu
Wang, Yue
Kadam, Shekhar
Show, Pau Loke
Ye, Xiaosong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, sonication (20-kHz) was conducted to assist the biosorption of phenolics from blueberry pomace extracts by brewery waste yeast biomass. The adsorption capacity of yeast increased markedly under ultrasonic fields. After sonication at 394.2 W/L and 40 °C for 120 min, the adsorption capacity was increased by 62.7% compared with that under reciprocating shaking. An artificial neural network was used to model and visualize the effects of different parameters on yeast biosorption capacity. Both biosorption time and acoustic energy density had positive influences on yeast biosorption capacity, whereas no clear influence of temperature on biosorption process was observed. Regarding the mechanism of ultrasound-enhanced biosorption process, the amino and carboxyl groups in yeast were considered to be associated with the yeast biosorption property. Meanwhile, ultrasound promoted the decline of the structure order of yeast cells induced by phenolic uptake. The interactions between yeast cells and phenolics were also affected by the structures of phenolics. Moreover, the mass transfer process was simulated by a surface diffusional model considering the ultrasound-induced yeast cell disruption. The modeling results showed that the external mass transfer coefficient in liquid phase and the surface diffusion coefficient under sonication at 394.2 W/L and 40 °C were 128.5% and 74.3% higher than that under reciprocating shaking, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Fruit pomace
Yeast biosorption
Phenolics
Neural network
Diffusional model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of phenolics from blueberry pomace
Determination of yeast particle size distribution
Experimental comparison of yeast biosorption kinetics with and without sonication
Biosorption isotherm of phenolics on brewery waste yeast at 40 °c
Characteristics of brewery waste yeast before and after biosorption
Changes of phenolic profiles in blueberry extracts after yeast biosorption
Effect of ultrasound treatment on the external mass transfer coefficient

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of biosorption kinetics of phenolics by brewery waste yeast with and without ultrasonic assistance at 40 °C. The lines represented the diffusional model.
Architecture and predictive accuracy of ANN model to predict yeast biosorption capacity as a function of AED level, temperature and biosorption time.
4D graph showing the effects of different parameters on the adsorption of phenolics on brewery waste yeast.
Biosorption isotherms of blueberry phenolics on brewery waste yeast at 40 °C. The lines represent the Langmuir isotherms.
Particle size distribution of yeast particles before and after biosorption of phenolics from blueberry pomace extract at 40 °C.
Scanning electron microscopy of brewery waste yeast before biosorption (a), yeasts after biosorption under reciprocating shaking (b), and yeasts after biosorption with sonication at 394.2 W/L (c).
FTIR spectra of yeast particles before and after biosorption of phenolics from blueberry pomace extract at 40 °C.
XRD patterns of brewery waste yeast cells before biosorption (a), yeast cells after biosorption under reciprocating shaking (b), and yeast cells after biosorption with sonication at 394.2 W/L (c).
Parameters of Langmuir isotherms for the biosorption of phenolics by brewery waste yeast cells at 40 °C.
Comparison in maximum adsorption capacities of phenolics assessed by Langmuir model in this study with the data reported in the literature.

row.entry: Phenolic mixture from Inga edulis leaves
Activated carbon coated with milk protein
Room temperature, reciprocating shaking at 150 rpm
Phenolic mixture from real wine wastewater

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Concentrations (mg/L) of specific phenolic compounds in blueberry phenolic extracts before and after 2-h yeast biosorption at 40 °C.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data were averages of different samples ± standard deviation.
Values followed by different letters (e.g., a, b, ab and others) in each line indicated significant differences (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: External mass transfer coefficient, surface diffusion coefficient of phenolics and accuracy of diffusional model for yeast biosorption at 40 °C.

head.ce:title: Parametric and phenomenological studies about ultrasound-enhanced biosorption of phenolics from fruit pomace extract by waste yeast

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing 210095, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kerry Global Technology and Innovation Centre, Naas, Co. Kildare W91W923, Ireland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kerry Global Technology and Innovation Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, University of Nottingham Malaysia Campus, Jalan Broga, Semenyih 43500, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhihai Postgraduate Working Station, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, sonication (20-kHz) was conducted to assist the biosorption of phenolics from blueberry pomace extracts by brewery waste yeast biomass. The adsorption capacity of yeast increased markedly under ultrasonic fields. After sonication at 394.2 W/L and 40 °C for 120 min, the adsorption capacity was increased by 62.7% compared with that under reciprocating shaking. An artificial neural network was used to model and visualize the effects of different parameters on yeast biosorption capacity. Both biosorption time and acoustic energy density had positive influences on yeast biosorption capacity, whereas no clear influence of temperature on biosorption process was observed. Regarding the mechanism of ultrasound-enhanced biosorption process, the amino and carboxyl groups in yeast were considered to be associated with the yeast biosorption property. Meanwhile, ultrasound promoted the decline of the structure order of yeast cells induced by phenolic uptake. The interactions between yeast cells and phenolics were also affected by the structures of phenolics. Moreover, the mass transfer process was simulated by a surface diffusional model considering the ultrasound-induced yeast cell disruption. The modeling results showed that the external mass transfer coefficient in liquid phase and the surface diffusion coefficient under sonication at 394.2 W/L and 40 °C were 128.5% and 74.3% higher than that under reciprocating shaking, respectively.

ce:def-description.ce:para: total phenolic concentration in liquid phase (mg/L)
initial concentration of total phenolics in liquid phase (mg/L)
total phenolic concentration at equilibrium in liquid phase (mg/L)
total phenolic concentration within the yeast particle at distance r (mg/L)
total phenolic concentration at the external surface of yeast particle at r = Rp (mg/L)
total phenolic concentration in liquid phase at time t (mg/L)
molecular diffusion coefficient at infinite dilution (cm2/s)
external mass transfer coefficient in liquid phase (cm/s)
mass of phenolics adsorbed by yeast (mg/g)
average value of mass of phenolics adsorbed by yeast for all the experimental data (mg/g)
mass of phenolics adsorbed at equilibrium (mg/g)
experimentally determined mass of phenolics adsorbed by yeast (mg/g)
predicted mass of phenolics adsorbed by yeast (mg/g)
external surface area per mass of adsorbent (m2/g)

ce:section.ce:para: Fruit pomace comprises residuals of fruit, such as skin, stalk, pulp and seeds. It is a byproduct of fruit juice, concentrate, wine and other processes [1]. The chemical composition of fruit pomace includes valuable phytochemicals and carbohydrates. Although a number of attempts have been made for their utilization, 80% of the generated pomace is still disposed simply by landfilling, animal feeding, composting and incineration [2]. Thus, better sustainable measures are required for valorization of fruit pomace, ultimately reducing the environmental impact.

The recovery of bioactive compounds, particularly phenolics, is an important method for the valorization of fruit pomace [3,4]. Phenolic compounds possess many bioactivities, which include anti-inflammatory, anti-carcinogenic, and analgesic activities. Epidemiological studies demonstrate that the consumption of phenolics can reduce the risk of many chronic diseases [5]. To date, many studies have reported the methods of extraction of phenolics from fruit pomace using ultrasound [3], supercritical fluid [6], and enzymes [7], etc. In contrast, there are limited reports on the separation and concentration of phenolics obtained from fruit pomace.

In recent years, various biological materials have been processed into biosorbents or used directly for the removal of target components from liquid solutions [8–13]. Among them, yeast biosorption has appeared as an emerging, cost effective and simple technology for the recovery of phenolics from a liquid medium [8,9]. A series of functional groups in the yeast cell wall such as lipids, proteins and polysaccharides can trigger the biosorption of metal ions, pigments and phenolics [8,14,15]. This biosorption property can be used for the concentration and separation of phenolics from the fruit pomace solutions. Moreover, the attachment of phenolic compounds to yeast cells can also prevent the loss of antioxidant capacity of phenolics during digestion and as a consequence increase their bioaccessibility [16]. Due to the aforementioned biosorption properties of yeast and the enhanced bioaccessibility of phenolics, the brewery waste yeast biomass has been used for the biosorption of anthocyanins from grape pomace [8,9]. The residual brewery yeast biomass (Saccharomycescerevisiae) could be a cheap and easily available adsorbent for the recovery of phenolics. Moreover, waste yeast biomass enriched with bioactives can be further processed into new value-added products.

Numerous studies have demonstrated that ultrasound can be used to accelerate adsorption uptake and kinetics in comparison with mechanical agitation [17,18]. The basis of ultrasonic enhancement of adsorption process is the ultrasound cavitation and the generated physical and chemical phenomena, such as locally extreme high pressure and temperature, microscopic turbulence, microstreaming, etc. [19]. The aforementioned ultrasonic factors can facilitate the transfer of adsorbate across liquid film surrounding adsorbent, hit adsorbent surface and create pores, expose more binding sites, strength the interactions between adsorbate and adsorbent, and consequently enhance the adsorption process [17,20]. In the case of the biosorption process, Kostić et al. [21] studied the effect of ultrasound power on the removal of Cu2 + by xanthated Lagenaria vulgaris shells. After a comprehensive literature survey, the application of ultrasound to assist in the biosorption of phenolics using yeast as adsorbent has not been reported yet. The aim of this study is to understand the application of ultrasound to enhance the yeast biosorption of phenolics from a liquid medium with respect to the effects of different parameters on yeast biosorption capacity, the mass transport mechanism and the binding features of yeasts with phenolic compounds under ultrasonic fields.

To study the influence of ultrasound treatment conditions on the capacity of yeast to adsorb phenolics, various parameters should be taken into consideration [17]. For this purpose, multivariate analysis is usually needed to investigate the influence of each single factor and the combined effects of different factors on the adsorption capacity of adsorbent simultaneously. Both response surface methodology and artificial neural network (ANN) can meet this requirement. However, ANN is a more powerful tool to combine dependent and independent variables together, and enables clearly visualizing the effects of all the factors on their own and when combined [22]. Meanwhile, the adsorption process usually includes three steps, namely external mass transport, intraparticle diffusion and adsorption on active sites distributed on the pore surface of adsorbents [23,24]. To accurately elucidate the mechanism about ultrasonic enhancement of mass transfer during yeast biosorption of phenolics from fruit pomace, the effect of ultrasound on all these three steps should be taken into consideration. For this purpose, the diffusional model can integrate different mass transfer steps during adsorption properly. By solving the diffusional model numerically, the detailed mass transfer mechanism of the adsorption process can be interpreted well from the estimated mass transfer parameters [24,25,26].

Overall, this study focused on the application of ultrasound to improve the biosorption of phenolics from fruit pomace by brewery waste yeast. Blueberry pomace was used as a representative fruit pomace material. The influences of different parameters on biosorption capacity were studied through ANN modeling. Meanwhile, numerical simulation method based on diffusional model was employed to clarify the effect of ultrasound on the mass transfer mechanism during yeast biosorption. Moreover, interactions between yeast and phenolics and the detailed phenolic composition in the liquid medium were analyzed to further explore the yeast biosorption mechanism under ultrasound fields. This work can provide fundamental knowledge about the ultrasound-assisted biosorption of phenolics from fruit pomace using yeast as adsorbent.

The blueberry pomace (Vaccinium ashei) was selected as a representative fruit pomace for all the experiments, which was kindly provided by Zelang Biotech Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). These pomace samples were the residues after blueberry juice processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of phenolics from blueberry pomace
ce:section.ce:para: Phenolics in blueberry pomace were extracted according to the method of Cui et al. [28] with some modifications. A known quantity of blueberry pomace was macerated by continuousmagnetic stirring in the dark at ambient temperature for 48 h. Aqueous ethanol (50%, pH 2.0) was used as solvent at a solid to solvent ratio of 1:15 (g:mL). After that, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to obtain supernatant rich in phenolic compounds. This phenolic extract was then stored at −20 °C.

The biosorption kinetic study was performed in a 100-mL Erlenmeyer flask containing 0.3 g of dried waste yeast particles and 50 mL of the aforementioned blueberry phenolic extract solution. The total phenolic concentration of this extract was adjusted by dilution to 1600 mg/L. This flask was then placed in a thermostatic water bath with an impeller (DC-0506, Fandilang Tech, Nanjing, China). An ultrasonic probe (VCX130, Sonics and Materials Inc., Newtown, USA) with a diameter of 1 cm and operational frequency of 20 kHz was then inserted into the dispersion. Meanwhile, the Erlenmeyer flask was sealed to avoid solvent evaporation during biosorption. This experimental setup is illustrated in Supplementary Fig. 1. Sonication was performed in a pulsed mode (on for 5 s and off for 5 s) and actual ultrasound power dissipated in the biosorption system was determined using the calorimetric method [29]. The total phenolic concentration in the solution was analyzed at the interval of 0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90 and 120 min.

The quantity of phenolics adsorbed on brewery waste yeast was calculated as [9]:
(1)
q =
( C
A , 0 -
C
A . t
)
m
V

The biosorption kinetic studies were performed at different acoustic energy density (AED, 199.4, 278.8 and 394.2 W/L) and temperature (20, 30 and 40 °C) levels. At the same time, a biosorption process with reciprocating shaking at 160 rpm and 40 °C was also conducted in a thermostatic shaking bath (SYC-C, Jingda Inc., Changzhou, China).

For isotherm study, 0.3 g of yeast particles was put in a 100-mL Erlenmeyer flask and 50 mL of blueberry phenolic extracts at five different concentrations were added. Then, ultrasound treatments at 199.4, 278.8 and 394.2 W/L were performed at 40 °C until the biosorption reached equilibrium (approximately 2 h). After reaching the equilibrium, the total phenolic concentration in the solution (CA, e) was determined spectrophotometrically and the quantity of phenolics adsorbed at equilibrium was calculated. The experimental data about CA
,e and qe were fitted to the Langmuir isotherm [30]:
(2)
q e
=
q m
b
C
A , e
1 + b
C
A , e

For the comparison purpose, the biosorption isotherm under reciprocating shaking at 160 rpm and 40 °C was also developed. The constants of the Langmuir isotherm were obtained by the nonlinear curve fitting method performed in MATLAB R2010a (The MathWorks, Inc., MA, USA).
The total phenolic content in the solution was determined by the Folin-Ciocalteu colorimetric method [31]. The results were expressed as mg/L of gallic acid equivalents.

The major phenolic compounds in the blueberry variety used in this study were previously identified by high-resolution mass spectrometry (data not shown). The changes of phenolic profile in the blueberry extracts before and after yeast biosorption were analyzed by HPLC according to the method of Tao et al. [32]. The quantification of each phenolic compound was made according to the calibration curve of the corresponding standard.

The anthocyanic phenolics were analyzed directly using an HPLC system (Agilent 1200; GMI, Ramsey, MN, USA) equipped with an Agilent TC-C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm). Mobile phase A was deionized water with 0.5% trifluoroacetic acid, and mobile phase B was acetonitrile. The elution profile was 0–5 min, 10–12% B; 5–14 min, 12–13% B; 14–16 min, 13–14% B; 16–18 min, 14–16% B; 18–19 min, 16–18% B; 19–22 min, 18–22% B; 22–35 min, 22–30% B. The detected wavelength was 520 nm and the column temperature was 30 °C. Furthermore, the flow rate was 0.8 mL/min and the injection volume was 20 μL.
Non-anthocyanic phenolics were first extracted from crude blueberry extracts before HPLC analysis.

For the extraction of phenolic acids, the pH value of phenolic extracts was adjusted to 2.0, in order to protonate free phenolates. Later, the suspension was saturated with NaCl. The portion of phenolic acid was extracted using ethyl acetate (1:1, v:v) for 12 h, and the entire procedure was repeated in triplicate. Collected 3 organic phase fractions were mixed together and the solvents were removed using rotary evaporation. The dried residues were dissolved in methanol and passed through a 0.1-μm filter. On the other hand, the pH value of crude extracts was adjusted to 7.0 before the extraction of flavonol portion. Flavonols were also extracted using ethyl acetate (1:1, v:v) for 12 h, and the extraction process was performed in triplicate. The organic layers were mixed, and solvents were then removed. The dried residues were suspended in methanol and filtered.

The contents of phenolic acids and flavonols were quantified in the aforementioned HPLC system equipped with an Agilent Zorbax Eclipse SB-C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm). Phenolic acids and flavonols were detected at 280 and 350 nm, respectively. Mobile phase A was deionized water with 1% trifluoroacetic acid, while mobile phase B was methanol with 1% acetic acid. A gradient program at a flow rate of 1 mL/min was used: 0–10 min, 10–26% B; 10–25 min, 26–40% B; 25–45 min, 40–65%; 45–55 min, 65–95% B; 55–58 min, 95–10% B; 58–61 min, 10% B. The injection volume was 20 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of yeast particle size distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The yeast particle size during biosorption was analyzed and monitored using a particle size analyzer (LS-C(III), OMEC, Zhuhai, China).

After biosorption, the brewery waste yeast was collected through filtration and then freeze-dried. The microstructure of brewery waste yeast before and after biosorption was investigated using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEM-5610LV, JEOL, Japan). Samples were attached to SEM tubes using double-sided adhesive tape and then coated with gold. SEM was operated at 15 kV and magnification of ×1000.

The FTIR analysis was conducted in the FTIR Nicolet IR200 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using the KBr disc method. The spectra were recorded in the transmission model from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of ±2 cm−1 at 21 scans/min.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) traces of yeast particles before and after biosorption were studied using an X-ray powder diffractometer (D/max 2500, Rigaku, Japan). The data were collected over an angular range from 3 to 70 2θ in continuous mode using a step size of 0.02 2θ and step time of 5 s.

For parametric study, a three-layered feed-forward artificial neural network was constructed to correlate the operating parameters, namely the AED level, temperature, and biosorption time with the quantity of phenolics adsorbed on yeast. Three nodes representing the AED level, temperature, and biosorption time were involved in the input layer. The output layer had one neuron denoting the yeast biosorption capacity. Only one hidden layer was used during ANN modeling to avoid the overfitting problem [33]. Moreover, the Marquardt-Levenberg learning algorithm was utilized for network training. The full cross-validation method was employed for data division during network training.

In the aforementioned network, the optimization of the topology of the ANN model was limited to the determination of the neuron number in the hidden layer, as well as the transfer functions used in both the hidden and output layers. The linear transfer function (purelin), log-sigmoid transfer function (logsig) and hyperbolic tangent sigmoid transfer function (tansig) were considered.

The predictive ability of the constructed ANN model for both training and cross-validation datasets was evaluated using three statistical indicators, including the coefficient of determination (R 2), root mean square error (RMSE), and absolute average deviation (AAD):
(3)
R

)
n
× 100

ANN modeling was performed in MATLAB, R2010a using the “newfit” function. Details about ANN modeling can be found in our previous works [33,34]. Once a robust ANN model was built, a 4D graph reflecting the influences of the AED level, temperature and biosorption time on the yeast biosorption capacity was generated using the “slice” function in MATLAB.

A diffusional model was used to clarify the mass transfer mechanism about ultrasound-enhanced biosorption of phenolics by brewery waste yeast. To build a realistic mass transfer model, the following phenomena were considered:
a.
External diffusion of phenolics across the liquid film surrounding yeast particles.
b.
Yeast adsorption of phenolics on the active sites.
c.
Intraparticle diffusion of phenolics within yeast particles by surface diffusion, pore diffusion or both.
d.
Ultrasound-induced disruption of yeast cells during biosorption.

For external mass transfer on the film surrounding yeast particles, it was supposed that mass transfer rate was proportional to concentration difference on the film, which can be described as:
(6)

)

Yeast particles were considered to have spherical geometries with symmetrical structure. Thus, the governing equation about intraparticle diffusion for a spherical particle is written as [35]:
(7)

The left side of Eq. (7) included the accumulation of phenolics in the pore volume of yeast particles, while the right side of Eq. (7) referred to both pore volume diffusion and surface diffusion.

The initial conditions are expressed as:

The adsorption rate on active sites was usually regarded to be instantaneous. Therefore, spontaneous equilibrium was achieved on the solid-liquid interface within the pore of the yeast. The relationship between total phenolic concentrations inside the yeast at any time and radical location and their concentration in liquid phase can be expressed by the adsorption isotherm [36]:
(13)
q = f (
C
A , r
)

On the other hand, the cavitation effect of ultrasound can damage the yeast cell structure, thus decreasing the moving distance of phenolics during the intraparticle diffusion stage [37]. For this reason, the mass transfer process was addressed as a moving boundary problem. Herein, an assumption was made that the yeast cell debris also had spherical geometry and the average particle size of yeast particles decreases linearly during sonication [37]. This relationship was used to quantify the moving distance of phenolics within yeast cells (yeast radius) when solving the diffusional model numerically.

The model described by Eqs. (7)–(12) was the general diffusional model. Due to the extremely low εp value mentioned previously, the Dep value was considered to be 0 according to the following relationship between εp and Dep , even if the τ value of the yeast samples was unknown:
(14)
D
ep
=
D
AB
ε p
τ

As a result, the diffusional model was reduced to a surface diffusional model (SDM) without pore diffusion. In practical applications, the pore volume diffusion is usually also ignored when surface diffusion occurs [38].

Prior to solving the diffusional model, the external mass transfer coefficient (kL ) was determined using the method of Furusawa and Smith [39]. When t approached 0, the Ca,r and Ca values were closed to 0 and Ca,
0, respectively. Meanwhile, the particle size and surface area of the yeast cells were assumed to be constant within a very short treatment time. If these conditions are substituted into Eq. (1), the following formula is obtained:
(15)
d
C A
C
A , 0
dt
t = 0
=
- m S
k L
V

The right side of Eq. (15) responded to the slope of phenolic concentration decay at t = 0. The value of k L was determined using the first two data points of concentration decay curve (t = 0 and t = 5 min).

The diffusional model was solved numerically using the pdepe function in MATLAB, since Eq. (7) was a partial differential equation in one space variable x and time t. The D s value was adjusted iteratively to fit the experimental points to minimize the RMSE value between experimental and calculated data:
(16)
RMSE =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
(
q
i , e
-
q
i , p
)
2

Biosorption kinetic and biosorption isotherm experiments were conducted in triplicate. The physicochemical analysis was performed in duplicate for each sample. The data about phenolic profile were analyzed by one-way analysis of variance in SPSS 11.5 (SPSS, Inc., USA). The significance was defined at p < 0.05 and the Least Significant Difference (Fisher’s LSD) test was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental comparison of yeast biosorption kinetics with and without sonication
ce:section.ce:para: The yeast biosorption of phenolics under an ultrasonic field was first compared with reciprocating shaking by a kinetic analysis. It can be seen from Fig. 1 that the quantity of phenolics adsorbed on yeast samples increased gradually before reaching the biosorption equilibrium, showing the binding capacity between brewery waste yeasts and blueberry phenolics [40]. Meanwhile, the biosorption capacity of yeast under an ultrasonic field was consistently higher than that of yeast under reciprocating shaking. For example, after sonication at 394.2 W/L for 120 min, the amount of phenolics adsorbed on waste yeast was 67.2 ± 1.0 mg/g, which was 62.7% higher than that adsorbed on yeast cells under reciprocating shaking. These results confirmed that ultrasound improved the efficiency of yeast biosorption of phenolics from fruit pomace extracts. Similarly, by using ultrasonic irradiation, Acisil et al. [41] and Low et al. [42] reported a significant increase in the adsorption efficiencies of montmorillonite and peanut husk powders to remove dyes in aqueous solution. The ultrasonic enhancement of yeast biosorption capacity can be attributed to the phenomena of acoustic cavitation and microstreaming, which may improve the adsorption process through promoting the movement of phenolic compounds across liquid film surrounding yeast particles, and strengthening their binding capacity with phenolic compounds and penetration into cells across the cell wall and membrane [20].

It needs to be mentioned that there might be the coagulation phenomena in the biosorption system if yeast cell concentration is high [43]. In this circumstance, the aggregation property of adsorbent may be proportional to their adsorption property [44]. However, sonication, especially at a relatively low energy level, can lead to the deflocculation of biological materials [45]. Meanwhile, according to a literature review, quite few papers considered the coagulation and adsorption behaviors simultaneously in a given biosorption process under ultrasonic fields. Thus, in the current study, it was tentatively exclude the influence of yeast coagulation on their adsorption capacity of phenolics. On the other hand, it is of interest to understand the exact relationship between yeast coagulation and adsorption properties in future studies.

Based on the neural network predictions, a 4D plot reflecting the variations of yeast biosorption capacity as a function of the independent variables was generated (Fig. 3 ). According to the color changes, the effects of ultrasound energy, temperature and biosorption time on the quantity of phenolics adsorbed by yeast cells can be easily visualized. Biosorption kinetic curves at different AED levels and temperatures are also plotted in Supplementary Fig. 2.

The quantity of adsorbed phenolics increased slowly after 80-min sonication as seen from the color change on the Z-axis in Fig. 3. The biosorption process could reach the equilibrium condition within the 2-h pulsed ultrasound treatment despite the ultrasound energy and temperature applied. Rubio et al. [8] reported that the equilibrium times for the biosorption of phenolics from grape pomace extracts by brewery waste yeast with and without chemical treatments were 4 and 6 h, respectively. By this comparison, it was further confirmed that ultrasound treatment could accelerate the yeast biosorption process. It was previously mentioned in Section 2.8.2 that the intraparticle diffusion was solely controlled by the surface diffusion. Accordingly, the biosorption of phenolics by yeast particles can be featured by the following steps: the migration of phenolics from liquid solution to the boundary layer of yeast particles; diffusion inside the yeast particles through surface diffusion instead of diffusion into biomass pores; biosorption to the active sites outside the cells [26]. All the three steps could be improved by applying sonication during biosorption.

According to the color change along with the X-axis in Fig. 3, it was found that the quantity of phenolics adsorbed on the yeast samples increased with the ultrasound energy dissipated in the system. Ultrasound energy is an important parameter for many ultrasound-included food processes and directly enhances food processes under investigation, except for fermentation [19,47]. In the case of the ultrasound-assisted yeast biosorption process, the increase of the AED level can generate stronger ultrasound cavitation, making the external diffusion of phenolic compounds across the liquid film surrounding yeast particles easier and exposing more binding sites on the yeast surface.

In this study, temperature had no clear influence on the yeast biosorption process as observed in Fig. 3. Similarly, Wang and Chen reported that a temperature between 5 and 40 °C had a minor effect on the biosorption of heavy metals by Saccharomyces cerevisiae [15]. On one hand, the biosorption process is an exothermic process. Thus, it could be favored by diminishing the temperature [48]. On the other hand, Goyal et al. [49] found that a rising temperature could strengthen the affinity between binding sites on yeast particles and the adsorbates. Most likely due to these contradictory effects of temperature, there was no explicit influence of temperature on the biosorption of phenolics from blueberry pomace extract by brewery waste yeast.

Moreover, there are other parameters that can affect the biosorption kinetics profoundly, including pH and the initial concentrations of both phenolics and yeast biomass. For future studies, the effects of these factors and their interaction effects need to be taken into consideration.

The phenomenological study was performed to investigate the effect of ultrasound on the binding behavior between yeast cells and phenolics, as well as reveal the mass transfer mechanism. All the phenomenological analyses were based on the biosorption processes conducted at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosorption isotherm of phenolics on brewery waste yeast at 40 °c
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between yeast cells and phenolics in the solution was first explored through an isotherm study. The Langmuir model was qualified to fit the experimental data (Fig. 4 ). The constants of the Langmuir isotherms are shown in Table 1 . The predicted q m value from the Langmuir model for biosorption under reciprocating shaking was 121.7 mg/g, while the obtained q m value for biosorption under sonication at 394.2 W/L increased to 186.1 mg/g. A substantial increase in the q m value also indicated the superiority of ultrasonic technology over mechanical agitation in improving yeast biosorption capacity.

The aforementioned q m values of phenolics onto brewery waste yeasts were furthermore compared with the corresponding q m values onto other adsorbents reported in recent years (Table 2 ). Although the adsorption capacity of a given adsorbent can be affected by various factors, as discussed in Section 3.2, it still can be concluded that brewery waste yeasts can be a competitive and low cost biosorbent for the recovery of phenolics from liquid solutions.

Based on these Langmuir isotherm parameters, a dimensionless constant of separation (RL ) was calculated as [9]:
(17)
R L
=
1
1 + b
C
A , 0

Herein, the RL values of biosorption processes conducted at 40 °C were 0.639 (reciprocating shaking), 0.632 (sonication at 199.4 W/L), 0.631 (sonication at 278.8 W/L) and 0.605 (sonication at 394.2 W/L). All these RL values are located between 0 and 1, implying that the yeast biosorption of phenolics with and without ultrasonic irradiation all occurred favorably [55]. Similarly, the biosorption of anthocyanins by Saccharomycescerevisiae yeast has been shown to be favorable [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of brewery waste yeast before and after biosorption
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size distributions of yeast particles before and after biosorption of phenolics from blueberry phenolic extracts are plotted in Fig. 5 . Generally, the control yeast and yeast after biosorption under reciprocating shaking had a wider particle size distribution, whereas the sizes of ultrasound-treated yeast were distributed in a narrower range. The mean particle sizes of control yeast, yeast after biosorption with reciprocating shaking, yeast sonicated at 199.4, 278.8 and 394.2 W/L were 7.44 ± 0.16, 8.60 ± 0.12, 4.74 ± 0.05, 4.64 ± 0.01 and 4.64 ± 0.10 μm, respectively. These results confirmed that sonication disrupted yeast cells, resulting in the decrease in average particle size. Meanwhile, due to the swelling effect [56], yeast particle size increased by 15.6% after 2-h biosorption with reciprocating shaking. The ultrasound-induced disruption of yeast cells could shorten the moving distance of phenolics within yeast cells and make the intraparticle diffusion easier, thus promoting the biosorption process.

The SEM images of yeast particles before and after biosorption are shown in Fig. 6 . It can be seen that yeast before biosorption mainly featured an oval shape with a smooth surface (Fig. 6a), while the phenomena regarding cell collapse and aggregation were observed after the adsorption of phenolics (Fig. 6b and c). Similar results were also reported in the study of Rubio et al. [8] regarding the yeast biosorption of antioxidants from grape pomace. Moreover, much yeast cell debris was found after sonication, which corresponded to the results regarding yeast particle size. Thus, it was necessary to take yeast cell disruption into account while simulating the yeast biosorption of phenolics under ultrasonic irradiation.

The FTIR analysis was performed to identify the functional groups in yeast cells relating to the biosorption process and investigate the biosorption mechanism from a chemical point of view. The FTIR spectra of control yeast and yeast after biosorption are illustrated in Fig. 7 . The control yeast exhibited a broad band near 3350 cm−1, which represented the vibration of hydroxyl group in carbohydrates and the presence of amino group in protein [9,14]. Meanwhile, the characteristic wavelength at 2930 cm−1 referred to the stretching of methyl and methylene groups in cell phospholipids [9]. More adsorption peaks were observed in the region from 1700 to 1000 cm−1. Specifically, the band at 1640 cm−1 was called the amide I band corresponding to the C = O stretching of protein bonds, while the small peak at 1585 cm−1 was the amide II band [57]. The intensive adsorption peak approximately 1400 cm−1 denoted the stretching of carboxyl groups in amide III [8]. Furthermore, the adsorption bands between 1250 and 1000 cm−1 (such as 1240 and 1047 cm−1) could be attributed to the complex vibrations from nucleic acid phospholipids, phosphodiester groups and carbohydrates in brewery waste yeast [9,14]. The details about the main adsorption bands and assignments in the FTIR spectrum of wine yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae can be found in the study of Burattini et al. [58].

Obvious changes in the FTIR spectrum were observed in the region from 1700 to 1300 cm−1 after the biosorption of blueberry phenolics. Compared with the spectrum of yeast before biosorption, there was a narrower but more intense adsorption peak at 1650 cm−1 in the spectra of yeast after biosorption with and without sonication. It showed that blueberry phenolics successfully attached on the yeast surface, since blueberry extracts had an adsorption band at this region due to the stretching of the aromatic CC groups of anthocyanins [34]. Meanwhile, the adsorption band corresponding to the amide II group in the yeast spectra moved to a lower frequency region (from 1585 to 1530 cm−1) after biosorption. This result was consistent with the finding reported by Stafussa et al. [9], which implied a chemical interaction between the yeast cell and blueberry phenolics. Moreover, the adsorption peak at approximately 1400 cm−1 that represented the stretching of carboxyl groups in amide III disappeared after 2-h biosorption. Overall, amino and carboxyl groups in yeast biomass were potentially involved in the interactions with blueberry phenolics.

There were no clear differences between the FTIR spectra of yeast treated by ultrasound and reciprocating shaking. Accordingly, it was supposed that no new binding sites on the yeast cell surface were exposed after sonication.

The XRD patterns of yeast particles before and after biosorption are illustrated in Fig. 8 . The X-ray diffractograms of all the yeast samples showed one intensive peak at approximately 20 degree demonstrating that these yeast particles were all in the amorphous phase [34]. In the meantime, the uptake of phenolics weakened the intensity of the main peak. The peak intensity for yeast samples after ultrasound-enhanced biosorption at 394.2 W/L was the lowest, while the peak intensity for yeast samples before biosorption was the highest. The attenuation of peak intensity can be attributed to incorporation of organic groups into yeast cells, since the presence of organic species can distort the regular arrangement and consequently, decrease the structure order [8]. These results suggested that adsorption of phenolics led to a less ordered structures of yeast cells and sonication intensified this reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes of phenolic profiles in blueberry extracts after yeast biosorption
ce:section.ce:para: In the studied blueberry variety, 6 anthocyanins, 4 phenolic acids and 3 flavonols were tentatively identified and their concentrations in blueberry extracts before and after 2-h yeast biosorption at 40 °C are listed in Table 3 . Generally, the studied blueberry variety had considerably high contents of malvidin-3-O-galactoside and malvidin-3-O-glucoside, which were consistent with the study of Avram et al. [59].

For analyzed phenolic compounds, their concentrations were the lowest in the liquid phase under sonication at 394.2 W/L, followed by sonication at 278.8 W/L and 199.4 W/L, and reciprocating shaking. These results confirmed that ultrasound enhanced the ability of yeast to retain phenolic compounds.

The type of phenolic compounds can affect the binding behavior of yeast. Taking sonication at 394.2 W/L for example, the concentration of malvidin-3-O-glucoside decreased by 34.4% after 2-h treatment, while the concentration of malvidin-3-O-galactoside was only 25.0% lower than its original concentration. For flavonols, 39.8% of hyperoside was adsorbed on yeast cells, the adsorption ratio of which was significantly (p < 0.05) higher than that of rutin (25.0%) and myricetin (24.2%). Other studies also revealed that the adsorption properties of phenolic compounds on yeast materials varied with their structures [60]. Thus, the influence of the structure of phenolics on their interactions with yeast cells needs to be clarified in further studies. Besides, the magnitudes of concentration decline for the four phenolic acids after 2-h biosorption were close to each other, ranging from 32.1% to 38.8%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasound treatment on the external mass transfer coefficient
ce:section.ce:para: The external mass transfer coefficients (k L) for total phenolics during yeast biosorption were calculated using the kinetic data within a 5-min biosorption period. During this period, the breaking up of yeast particles by ultrasound was neglected. The estimated k L values of total phenolics are listed in Table 4 . As can be seen, the k L values during biosorption with and without sonication ranged from 6.88 × 10−3 to 15.72 × 10−3 cm/s, which were in a similar range for pyridine adsorption on activated carbon reported by Ocampo-Perez et al. [25]. The kL values of ultrasound-enhanced biosorption processes were all higher than that under reciprocating shaking. At the same time, the external mass transfer coefficient increased with the input ultrasound energy. Generally, the external mass transfer during biosorption was strongly dependent on mechanical agitation intensity in the solution [38]. Compared to reciprocating shaking, ultrasound treatment could produce violent turbulence in the biosorption system, consecutively decreasing external mass transfer resistance.

The physics behind yeast biosorption under sonication was further explored by diffusion modeling. The simplified diffusional model, namely an SDM model considering yeast disruption factor was fitted to the biosorption kinetic data and the Ds value was obtained by matching the numerical solution of the SDM model to the experimental results. The optimal Ds values and the statistical indicators of the SDM model are summarized in Table 4. Meanwhile, the experimental versus predicted biosorption capacities under different treatments are graphed in Fig. 1. Overall, the SDM model fitted the experimental data with satisfactory accuracy since the AAD values were all lower than 10% and the R 2 values were higher than 0.95.

The Ds values for total phenolics during yeast biosorption at 40 °C remained in the range from 1.01 × 10−11 to 1.76 × 10−11 cm2/s. No studies previously reported the diffusion properties of phenolic compounds within yeast particles. However, the Ds values for phenolics identified in this study were smaller than Ds values for other compounds reported in the literature by several orders of magnitude. For example, the Ds values for phenol in organobentonite varied from 4.05 × 10-8 to 5.77 × 10-8 cm2/s [38]. The Ds values for food dyes in chitosan films were in the range of 10-10 cm2/s [61]. In comparison with porous adsorbent materials, the solid structure of yeast cells made the intraparticle diffusion process more difficult. Moreover, the Ds values for phenolics under ultrasound fields were all higher than that under reciprocating shaking. The Ds value under sonication at 394.2 W/L was 74.3% higher than that under reciprocating shaking. Since Ds was mainly affected by interactions between adsorbate molecules and the adsorbent surface [38], it can be concluded that ultrasound enhanced the interactions between phenolics and yeast particles to promote the diffusion process.

The application of ultrasound can clearly enhance the biosorption of phenolics from fruit pomace by brewery waste yeast. The ANN results revealed that yeast biosorption capacity increased with the AED level, while the effect of temperature on biosorption process was not clear. The adsorption properties of yeast biomass depended on the type of phenolics. Malvidin-3-O-glucoside had stronger interactions with yeast than cyanidin-3-O-glucoside. The FTIR results implied that amino and carboxyl groups in yeast may be involved in the interactions between yeast and phenolics from fruit pomace. The mass transfer process of phenolics during yeast biosorption was successfully simulated by a diffusional model incorporated with the yeast disruption factor. The mass transfer parameters, namely, k L and Ds , increased with the AED level, implying that ultrasound can enhance both the movement of phenolics in the liquid phase and the surface diffusion of phenolics in yeast cells.

In practical applications, ultrasound can be used to enhance the separation efficiency of phenolics from a liquid solution when brewery waste yeast biomass is utilized as biosorbent. Ultrasound is helpful for producing yeast biomass enriched with bioactive phenolics, which can be further processed into value-added products.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31701616), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu, China (Grant No. BK20170714), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. KJQN201824), Shuangchuang Talent Project, Jiangsu, China and Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund (Grant No. CX(18)2017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2018.11.018.
